text: I build object-based exhibits and digital storytelling that turn artifacts, archives, and the occasional ghost into public education people remember. MA candidate in Public History, CU Denver — Certified Interpretive Guide.
One built for hands and one built for screens — both built around a single interpretive idea.
Why does gear change over time?

An object-based gallery on 150 years of firefighter safety gear, built as an educational intern. Visitors try on real gear across eras — the adult panels cover OSHA history and Heather Larson, the department's first female captain.
Families, K–8 school groups, general visitors
Corner installation — gear try-on stations + five adult panels

A digital exhibit tracing Spiritualism's role in the 1848 origins of American feminism — from Amy Post's parlor to the movement's own erasure of the women who built it. Includes an interactive social-network graph visitors can redraw in real time.
Classmates, plus the general public via ArcGIS
Long before the MA, I was already doing versions of this work — the shape is on the left, the story is on the right.
Bars show duration; dots mark a single moment. Height is to scale — most recent at top, 2007 at bottom.
Supporting undergraduate history instruction. Providing feedback on student writing.
My immediate goal is to learn more about creating multimedia historical content that educates, engages, and broadens perspectives.
Historical Studies Journal — Editor, then Chief Editor
Reviewing and editing submissions for CU Denver's student-run, peer-reviewed history journal, publishing since 1986, then stepping up to lead the graduate editorial board as chief editor.

Contributed to education programming including storytimes for younger children, STEM education for older children, and outreach programs. Designed and installed the exhibit "Built to Protect," an object-based learning experience about the development of safety equipment over time.

My passion for history remained unfulfilled in my professional work, so in 2019 I started "Unlikely Explanations." It let me connect with my love of interpretation and conducting original research while still creating.

Executing long-term content strategies with digital marketing agencies and their clients, including high-tech, B2B, and real estate clients. Using search data to identify audience opportunities and creating targeted content for search niches.
Obtained as a first step toward transitioning into public history.

Led 50+ subject matter experts to create weekly articles that drove inbound traffic, leads, and conversion. Developed product pages, email campaigns, videos, guides, and webinars for quarterly product releases.
We Cryptids: An Urban Fantasy Anthology · 2021
Integer Group — Technical Copywriter, then Copywriter

Developed e-commerce content for national and multinational clients, including Starbucks, P&G, and Mars/Wrigley. Created product pages, landing pages, case studies, e-storefronts, videos, social posts, email campaigns, and banner ads. Managed freelance writers and studio designers.
Managed 500+ page manuals for telecommunication software. Worked with software engineers to keep documentation up to date.
I discovered I had accumulated enough history credits for a second major while completing my English Literature degree.
The timeline and the exhibit previews are the short version. Here's where the rest of the argument lives.
Big Idea, planning, concept & content, design, and install/outcome — for both Built to Protect and Speaking Through the Dead, with exhibit reproductions alongside commentary.
The full throughline in prose, and a skills-bridge table mapping fifteen years of content-marketing work onto museum practice.
Content Strategist → Public Historian · Denver, Colorado

People learn more, and more honestly, outside a classroom than in one — in a museum corner, on a podcast, scrolling a page they didn't have to click. Fifteen years of professional writing taught me how to hold someone's attention without a syllabus. A Master's in Public History gave that instinct a name: informal learning. I'm not leaving what I've done. I'm pointing it at something I actually believe in.

Fifteen years of professional writing taught me the same lesson in four different jobs: know who's reading, then write for them. Five hundred pages of telecom documentation at Transaction Network Services had to work for engineers who needed it accurate, not impressed. E-commerce copy for Starbucks, P&G, and Mars/Wrigley at Integer Group had to work on a stranger scrolling past it. Fifty-plus subject matter experts at Netgain needed weekly content that justified itself in real inbound traffic. Different audiences, same instinct: translate expert knowledge for someone who didn't ask for a lecture.

In 2019, I started [Unlikely Explanations](https://www.unlikelyexplanation.com/) because that instinct had no outlet in the day job. I wanted to do original historical research and tell people about it — pieces like "Why Did Steamboat Springs Blow Up The Steamboat Spring?," which used a local land dispute to explore differing Ute and white-settler attitudes toward the land itself, and "How Do We Cope With Gender?," which traced eunuchs, proto-trans figures, and third genders across history. It wasn't a career move. It was the first time in a decade that writing felt like the point instead of the deliverable.

Through the interviews I did for those stories — including one with Dr. Michael Barkun about his book *A Culture of Conspiracy* — I kept running into the same idea: that educators can act as a kind of inoculation against radicalization and bigotry, simply by giving people a place to encounter complexity instead of certainty. That's when public history stopped being a hobby and started being the plan. A Certified Interpretive Guide credential in 2022 was the first formal step. An MA in Public History at CU Denver, starting in 2025, is the real one.

[Built to Protect](__EXHIBIT_URL__#built-to-protect) and [Speaking Through the Dead](__EXHIBIT_URL__#speaking-through-the-dead), on the Exhibits page, are the proof this actually transfers — that the same instinct behind fifteen years of audience-first writing is the instinct behind a good museum label. This page is where I say the belief behind it outright: I think people learn best when nobody's making them, and I want to spend my career building the places where that happens.
What fifteen years of writing actually taught me

**Technical writing for engineers**
Transaction Network Services · 2011–2013
Working with subject-matter experts — curators, historians — to keep interpretive content accurate without losing the audience.

**E-commerce copywriting for national & multinational clients**
Integer Group · 2014–2019
Audience-level translation of expert content — the exact skill behind every object label and panel in *Built to Protect*.

**Leading 50+ subject matter experts to a publishing deadline**
Netgain Solutions · 2021–2022
Coordinating a graduate editorial board, or a multi-station exhibit install, toward one fixed public deadline.
The same instinct behind the *Built to Protect* prototype test — the original mechanism got cut because families weren't engaging with it, not because the research was wrong.
Exhibit No. 01 — Denver Firefighters Museum — Educational Intern, 2026
"Engineering is never finished. Every solution is the starting point for the next problem."
Families, K–8 school groups, general visitors
Corner installation — helmet, jacket, and mask/SCBA try-on stations, a drawing activity, and five adult panels

"Engineering is never finished" is one idea at two reading levels: the line an adult reads on a data panel, and "Safety Isn't Solved" for a kid pulling on a helmet. Both point at the same lesson — how engineering actually works, not just facts about firefighting gear.
Before drafting a single label, I grounded the design in three research frameworks — color-coded below, and again wherever they show up on the wall:
Show information more than one way: visually, tactilely, through data. **Here:** the same Big Idea reaches a visitor as a try-on prompt, a printed question, and a data panel, not just one of those.

Lead with the object, layer the labels — tombstone, thematic, extended — so the object generates the question before any text answers it. **Here:** every station puts a real or replica object in reach first; adult panels layer from headline down to full data for the visitor who wants to go deeper.

The Exploratorium's 9 design attributes proven to engage girls in STEM exhibits without alienating boys. **Here:** two-person try-on stations, open questions with no single right answer, and Heather Larson featured as a named protagonist rather than a footnote.

The research held up; the original mechanism didn't. I first designed four stations, each built around a flip-panel "decision tree" — a real historical fork, three possible paths, a reveal connecting the choice to what actually happened. I printed one full-size and watched families use it in the gallery. By that point in a visit, they already had **reading fatigue** from the rest of the museum — what worked was **handling the objects** and **talking to each other**, not solving a text puzzle. So I changed the mechanism and kept the Big Idea.

Same Big Idea, built twice for two different readers standing in the same corner. **Kid stations** run on EDGE's body-movement and collaboration engagement — do first, ask questions, no reading required to participate. **Adult panels** run on Object-Based Learning's layered, extended-interpretation labels — for the visitor with time to read data and history.
One of EDGE's 9 attributes: whole-body interaction, not just hands-only manipulation. Pulling on the coat is the whole prompt — you feel the weight before you read a word about it.
EDGE design meant for two people at once, not a solo visitor. A parent reading "why do you think the jackets are so heavy" out loud is already talking with their kid about it.

EDGE's term for labels that tell visitors what to **do**, not just what something is, so nobody has to walk up cold. The printed questions tell a kid what to look for and give a parent something to say about it.
OBL's central rule: let the object raise the question before the label answers it. You feel the jacket's weight and stiffness first — the words about turnout gear come after.

OBL calls for positioning an object "at eye level, well-lit, with enough space to see it from multiple angles" before a visitor reads anything. This station is mounted at a child's eye level, not an adult's — a real, physical decision about who it's built for.

OBL asks for "conversational tone... tested by reading aloud." The jacket talks in first person — "My jacket has thick canvas..." — at about a 4th-grade level, so a parent isn't stuck translating a label into kid-language on the spot. Same instinct behind [fifteen years of writing e-commerce copy for Starbucks, P&G, and Mars/Wrigley](__ABOUT_URL__#skills-bridge) — know who's reading, then write for them — aimed here at a nine-year-old instead of a shopper.
This station is the direct answer to what the prototype test found: ask the visitor to do something before asking them to read anything.
Two helmets, a century apart — same open questions.
Old beard-filter vs. modern air tank, side by side.
A blank page and one open prompt: what would you fix?
Exhibit, as installed — the wall's opening argument

OBL's middle label tier connects several objects to one shared question, for a visitor who wants to understand rather than keep moving. Every station on this wall is really making the same argument: why does gear change?

UDL's first principle: show the same idea more than one way. The kid stations argue "safety isn't solved" through touch; these panels make the same argument with history and data instead. Even the old-vs-new pairing repeats — the helmet and jacket become a room that burns in 29 minutes instead of 4.

OBL asks for concrete language over abstraction. "29 minutes to 4 minutes" and a cited 1971 NFPA mandate are both checkable, sourced right on the panel — numbers a visitor can picture instead of a vague claim about things getting more dangerous.
Same OBL positioning rule as the kid stations, at a different height. These panels sit at an adult's eyeline — the exhibit repositions itself physically depending on who's standing in front of it.
Look & Feel, Tuned for the Reader

EDGE's "inviting aesthetic" attribute isn't one fixed look. The kid stations use a warm vintage illustration; these panels use flat icons — a flame, a "Safety First" badge, a burst of smoke. Same attribute, a different visual language for a different reader.

These panels work two ways: something for an adult to relay back to a kid about gear, engineering, and change ("hey, did you know..."), or a complete read for someone who came in without one. Collaboration is offered here, not required.
Between the two, this is where the Big Idea gets stated outright: one panel says why gear keeps changing, the other proves it with a room going from 29 minutes to 4.
Smoke Eating: What Is Acceptable Danger?
Why firefighters once called themselves "smoke eaters" — and what it cost them.
How safety went from "writing rules in blood" to research-driven.
Her fight against a rigged strength test, and the data on what's still unequal.
The corner as installed — the full run of panels and gear together.

Every framework here is pointing at the same move: get out of the way and let the object make the argument. The jacket doesn't need a paragraph about why it's heavy — a kid finds that out with their own arms, then asks why. Object-Based Learning, Universal Design for Learning, and EDGE's body-movement attribute all land on that same design choice independently.

That's the real difference between the four-station concept I started with and what's on the wall now — not a smaller idea, just a shorter path between the visitor and the object doing the convincing.
Exhibit No. 02 — ArcGIS StoryMaps — Survey of Feminist Thought, CU Denver, 2026
The exhibit's own on-screen subtitle — one audience, not two reading levels the way Built to Protect splits by age
Classmates, plus the general public via ArcGIS
ArcGIS StoryMaps, with custom Flourish network-graph and Sankey-diagram embeds
A history feminism used, then buried

Spiritualism was a tool feminism **"embraced, profited from, and disposed of."** The exhibit makes that case through three stops and three interactives, not a lecture — built on the same three frameworks as Built to Protect, adapted for a digital, single-audience exhibit.

"Equitable learning environments guide learners... by valuing and cultivating multiple ways of knowing and making meaning" (CAST, 2024). **Here:** the one framework the reflection paper names outright — every stop and interactive offers more than one way in, unpacked below.

Lead with real evidence, then let prose explain it. **Here, without a physical object:** a primary source or archival image stands in for it — a substitution that shows up twice below, in two different forms.

Name a protagonist instead of a footnote, and ask questions with no single right answer instead of handing over a conclusion. **Here:** that shows up twice below too — once in who carries the story, once in how it's asked.

Same three frameworks, split by means instead of by audience this time. **The Narrative** — text and primary-source images, read in sequence — carries the argument the way a museum label does. **The Interactives** — a map, a network graph used twice, and a flow diagram — let a visitor test that same argument themselves.
read the argument, one stop at a time
Exhibit, as published — Stop One: The Parlor as Homosocial War Room

Amy Post opens the exhibit as a real person — a Rochester Quaker who hosted the Fox sisters, sheltered Harriet Jacobs, and anchored a reform network from her own parlor. Leading with a character gives a visitor someone to follow before any thesis shows up. She reappears, repositioned, in the network graph below.
UDL's first principle: show the same idea more than one way. This stop pairs a period portrait, narrative text, and an archival photograph of the actual parlor — three routes into the same claim.
My own line from the reflection paper: the exhibit "works at different levels of attention — from a casual five-minute glance or a fifteen-minute reflection to an hour-long exploration."

UDL's engagement guideline calls for learner autonomy and choice, not just varied formats. My own phrase: the exhibit "encourages learner agency by declining to collapse the story of American feminism into a respectable teleology" — no single correct reading is handed to the visitor.

OBL's central rule, adapted: let the real thing carry the claim before prose explains it. There's no physical object here, so a primary-source photograph and Smith-Rosenberg's scholarship do that work instead — evidence first, argument second.

The same EDGE move as Heather Larson in Built to Protect: Amy Post gets a name, a face, and a story, not a line in a list of "women involved." A visitor follows a person, then learns the argument through her.

EDGE's labels-that-tell-you-what-to-do principle, in the same open-ended form as Built to Protect's kid-register prompts: "What do you notice? Who remains?" has no single right answer — it invites a look, not a correct response.
Spiritualism turned the era's own stereotypes of feminine nervousness into a tool for public authority.
A Legal Cover for Public Speech
Trance channeling let a woman speak in public without the shame 19th-century religion attached to it — a real loophole, not a metaphor.
White Spiritualists claimed Native and Asian "spirit guides" as a source of authority — a real historical wrong the exhibit names directly, not around.
Exhibit, as published — the social network graph, used twice

The same tool, reused with different labels, turns a historiographical point into something a visitor watches happen instead of reads. Relabel the rings, and the network redraws itself — Amy Post moves to the outer edge, Elizabeth Cady Stanton moves to the center. One visualization, two arguments, no new build.
My own line: the interactives "communicate on three registers: gestalt impact, text labels, and interactive experience" — a visitor absorbs the shape of the argument before ever clicking a name.
A quick glance at the toggle reads the argument at a distance; clicking through every biography reads it in depth. Both are legitimate ways to use the same tool.

A specific UDL checkpoint under Comprehension: help a learner apply a pattern they've already learned to something new. A visitor who's figured out "closer to center means more central" reads the second, relabeled version instantly — same skill, new argument, no re-teaching.

OBL calls for concrete, checkable, sourced content over abstraction. The relabeled rings aren't invented categories — "Erased Entirely," "Stripped of Spiritualism," "Minimized" come straight out of Braude, Smith-Rosenberg, and Lorde's scholarship on how this erasure actually happened.

UDL's action-and-expression guidelines call for optimizing access to the tools themselves, not just offering several. My own footnote names the real gap plainly: "there are some technical problems with this interactive... the tag toggling would be more intuitive... with a single click."

Click a node and the sidebar goes deeper — name and dates first (tombstone), then tags (thematic), then a full paragraph (extended). The same three-tier layering as Built to Protect's OBL cards, rebuilt for a click instead of a wall.
A five-stop pin map tracing Spiritualism's spread from the Fox sisters' Hydesville farmhouse toward Seneca Falls.
Which movements' beliefs counted as legitimate, charted as its own axis of exclusion alongside race, class, and sexuality.
The exhibit's closing move — a slider between the respectable public face of suffrage and the séance it grew out of.

UDL, OBL, and EDGE are three different vocabularies for the same instinct here: hand a visitor real evidence and more than one way into it, then get out of the way. A named person instead of a name in a list. A primary source instead of a paragraph summarizing it. A toggle to test instead of a conclusion to accept. That's the instinct behind Built to Protect's jacket station too, where a kid feels the weight of the coat before reading a word about why it matters.

That instinct holds up across very different conditions: a fifteen-foot museum corner and a scrolling web page, a nine-year-old visitor and a college classroom, a physical jacket and a social-network graph. The framework changes with the medium. The underlying design judgment doesn't.
"We can better understand our history if we find our own path through a world that is messier, stranger, and more inclusive than the story that society prefers to tell about itself."
